notes.note: When both consulships fell vacant, the Senate appointed a series of interreges, each holding office for five days, to conduct elections. In 53 BC electoral violence produced a long string of these interregna, the basis of Cicero's joke that no one needs a jurist.

notes.anchor: demand two adjournments from each interrex
notes.note: A mock legal tip: an advocatio was a postponement of a case. Cicero pretends that the rapid turnover of interreges can be exploited to stall any prosecution indefinitely, parading the 'civil law' he claims to have learned from Trebatius.

Decimus Laberius, the leading writer of mimes (low comic sketches) of the day. Cicero jokes that if Trebatius stays away too long he will become a stock comic type, 'the British jurisconsult,' fit for Laberius's stage.
Cicero's running gag: Trebatius, a serious Roman jurist, has been reduced to dispensing law in the wilds of Gaul and Britain, an absurd figure ripe for the comic stage.
